{"id":"spgr-jpp.2","title":"UpdateDecision passes string values where *string is expected, losing nil-field semantics","description":"In /internal/server/decision_handler.go:UpdateDecision (line 4891), the handler calls:

  h.store.UpdateDecision(ctx, msg.Slug, msg.Title, msg.Status, msg.Decision, msg.Rationale, msg.SupersededBy)

The storage interface (storage/decision.go) expects (ctx, slug string, title, status, decision, rationale, supersededBy *string), but msg.Title, msg.Status etc. are protobuf optional fields — the generated getter returns a plain string (not *string). As written the call does not compile unless the proto field is directly a *string.

Inspecting the generated code (decision.pb.go line 1852-1856), Title/Status/Decision/Rationale/SupersededBy are actually *string in the proto struct, so the getters return string but the raw fields are *string. The handler calls the getters (which return string), so it passes strings, not *string, causing a type mismatch that will prevent compilation.

Fix: access the raw pointer fields directly (msg.Title, msg.Status etc. are already *string in the generated struct when using proto optional), or use the pointer fields: pass &msg.Title only when changed is relevant. The correct approach is to pass msg.Title (as *string) by reading the struct field rather than the getter method.

If the code compiles today it may be because the getter returns the pointer — verify the generated code accessor signatures are consistent with what the handler passes.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-01T00:37:44Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-01T00:37:44Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/pull/4"}

{"description":"ClaimSpecRequest.LeaseDuration and HeartbeatRequest.ExtendBy (proto/specgraph/v1/claim.proto and gen/specgraph/v1/claim.pb.go) accept any google.protobuf.Duration value including zero, negative, or arbitrarily large values. The only invariant is a comment '(default 15m)'.

In internal/server/claim_handler.go lines 4647 and 4667:
  claim, err := h.store.ClaimSpec(ctx, msg.Slug, msg.Agent, msg.LeaseDuration.AsDuration())
  claim, err := h.store.Heartbeat(ctx, msg.Slug, msg.Agent, msg.ExtendBy.AsDuration())

Note: durationpb.Duration.AsDuration() is nil-receiver safe (returns 0), so no panic. However, a zero or negative duration is silently accepted and passed to the storage layer, which then falls back to the 15m default. This means the request field has no enforced invariant — a caller sending -1s or 0 gets a silent default substitution with no error or warning.

Additionally, no upper bound is enforced. A caller could request a 10-year lease duration, which the storage layer would accept.

Suggested fix: Validate at the handler layer that LeaseDuration/ExtendBy, when non-nil/non-zero, is positive and within a reasonable maximum. Return CodeInvalidArgument if out of range. This makes the invariant explicit and gives callers actionable errors rather than silent substitution."}

The handler passes msg.Intent etc. which are *string — this is correct. The storage backend receives nil for unset fields and non-nil for set fields as intended. No bug here.","status":"closed"}

{"id":"spgr-jpp.8","title":"UpdateSpec handler passes proto optional fields as *string to storage, but handler gets non-pointer values from GetX() accessors","description":"In internal/server/spec_handler.go lines ~5580-5591, UpdateSpec passes msg.Intent, msg.Stage, msg.Priority, msg.Complexity directly to h.backend.UpdateSpec(), which expects *string for each field (only update non-nil fields). However msg.Intent etc. are retrieved via the generated proto getter (GetIntent()/GetStage() etc.), which returns a plain string (not *string) — a non-nil string even when the field was not set in the request.

The proto optional fields (UpdateSpecRequest.Intent is 'optional string') generate two things: a *string field and a GetIntent() accessor that dereferences it (returning "" for nil). So the handler always passes non-nil strings to the storage backend, meaning every UpdateSpec call overwrites ALL fields — even those the caller did not set. This defeats the entire partial-update design.

The same bug exists in DecisionHandler.UpdateDecision at internal/server/decision_handler.go line ~4891: it passes msg.Title, msg.Status, etc. (plain strings via GetX()) rather than the underlying *string pointer fields.

Root cause: The handlers should pass msg.Title (the *string pointer field) rather than msg.GetTitle() (the string accessor). Proto3 optional fields expose both a *string field (for nil-checking) and a GetX() string accessor. The handlers use the accessor, collapsing the optional semantics.

Fix (spec_handler.go UpdateSpec):
  h.backend.UpdateSpec(ctx, msg.Slug, msg.Intent, msg.Stage, msg.Priority, msg.Complexity)
should be:
  h.backend.UpdateSpec(ctx, msg.Slug, msg.Intent, msg.Stage, msg.Priority, msg.Complexity)
where msg.Intent is the *string field directly (since proto3 optional generates a pointer field named exactly Intent, not just a getter). Accessing req.Msg.Intent directly (not req.Msg.GetIntent()) gives the *string.

This is a silent correctness bug: partial updates silently become full overwrites, setting unspecified fields to empty string.","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-01T00:38:06Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-01T00:38:06Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/pull/4"}

{"id":"spgr-jpp.46","title":"Storage interface mixes proto types and storage-layer types inconsistently across services","description":"The storage interfaces exhibit an inconsistent coupling pattern between proto types and storage-layer types:

- ClaimBackend (internal/storage/claim.go) returns *specv1.Claim — a proto type — directly from storage.
- GraphBackend (internal/storage/graph.go) returns []storage.NodeRef (a storage-layer type) for query methods, but returns *specv1.Edge (a proto type) for AddEdge/ListEdges.
- DecisionBackend (internal/storage/decision.go) returns *specv1.Decision — a proto type.
- Backend (internal/storage/storage.go) returns *specv1.Spec — a proto type.

This means the storage layer has a mandatory compile-time dependency on the generated proto package (specgraphv1). If the proto shape changes (field additions, renames), the storage interface breaks — even for backends that would otherwise be unaffected.

The NodeRef case (storage.NodeRef being a storage-native type) in GraphBackend is inconsistent with the other backends and requires a manual conversion step in graph_handler.go (nodeRefsToProto). This inconsistency makes it unclear whether storage types should be proto-independent or not.

Suggested fix: Either (a) make all storage interfaces proto-independent by returning storage-native structs throughout, or (b) accept the proto coupling and remove the storage.NodeRef indirection (return *specv1.NodeRef directly from GraphBackend). The current mixed approach is the worst of both worlds: it incurs the indirection cost without achieving the isolation benefit.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-01T00:38:55Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-01T00:38:55Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/pull/4"}

{"id":"spgr-jpp.36","title":"UpdateSpec and UpdateDecision use CodeNotFound for all storage errors","description":"In internal/server/spec_handler.go UpdateSpec handler (line 5588) and internal/server/decision_handler.go UpdateDecision handler (line 4893), all storage errors are mapped to connect.CodeNotFound regardless of error type. If the storage layer returns an internal/infrastructure error (e.g., database connection lost), clients receive a 404-equivalent response and will incorrectly retry as if the resource does not exist rather than treating it as a server-side failure. Fix: distinguish between not-found (CodeNotFound) and other errors (CodeInternal) by checking error type/sentinel from the storage layer, or by having storage return typed errors.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-01T00:38:43Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-01T00:38:43Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/pull/4"}

{"id":"spgr-jpp.26","title":"UpdateSpec and UpdateDecision return CodeNotFound for all errors, hiding internal failures","description":"In /internal/server/spec_handler.go:UpdateSpec (line 5587) and /internal/server/decision_handler.go:UpdateDecision (line 4893), all storage errors are mapped to CodeNotFound:

  return nil, connect.NewError(connect.CodeNotFound, err)

A database connection failure, a timeout, or a query error will be reported to the client as 404 Not Found. This makes debugging harder and causes clients to retry unnecessarily (404 is not retryable, while CodeInternal or CodeUnavailable signals a transient failure).

The correct pattern (already used in CreateDecision, ListDecisions, etc.) is to return CodeInternal for storage errors and only CodeNotFound when the record is explicitly absent. The two handlers should inspect the error or use a sentinel error value:
  - Use CodeInternal for unexpected storage errors
  - Use CodeNotFound only when the error message/type indicates the entity was not found

Consider introducing a typed error (e.g., storage.ErrNotFound) to allow handlers to discriminate.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-01T00:38:32Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-01T00:38:32Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/pull/4"}

{"id":"spgr-jpp.35","title":"init.go runInit uses global cfgFile without parent directory creation, panics when path does not exist","description":"In /cmd/specgraph/init.go:runInit (line 721), the os.Stat check uses the cfgFile path as-is without creating the parent directory, but cfg.Write already calls os.MkdirAll (config.go line 6432), so this is handled. However, the test in init_test.go (line 797-798) uses path '.specgraph/config.yaml' inside a temp dir, while TestInitAlreadyExists uses 'config.yaml' directly. These two tests use different path depths, so one tests directory creation and the other does not — the coverage is asymmetric.

More importantly, there is no validation of the user-supplied backend or mode values. The interactive path accepts any string:
  cfg.Storage.Backend = backend  // could be 'invalid_backend'
  cfg.Server.Mode = mode          // could be 'invalid_mode'

These invalid values propagate to runServe where the switch on cfg.Storage.Backend falls through to a non-existent case and the server silently fails to register the backend.

Fix: validate backend and mode inputs in runInit against the known valid values (memgraph, postgres for backend; docker, external for mode) and return an error for unknown values.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-01T00:38:42Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-01T00:38:42Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/pull/4"}

{"id":"spgr-jpp.23","title":"Multiple init() functions per file — fragmented registration pattern","description":"Several CLI files split their flag/command registration across multiple init() functions — one per sub-command — when a single init() would be clearer and is the Go convention.

Affected files:
- cmd/specgraph/claim.go: two separate init() blocks (lines 46-51 and 83-87)
- cmd/specgraph/decision.go: four separate init() blocks (lines 173-175, 192-198, 228-231, 270-272)
- cmd/specgraph/edge.go: four separate init() blocks

Each block registers exactly one sub-command plus its flags. Consolidating into a single init() per file makes the full set of sub-commands visible at a glance and matches Go idiomatic style.

Example (cmd/specgraph/claim.go):
Before — two separate init() functions.
After:
func init() {
    claimCmd.Flags().StringVar(&claimAgent, "agent", "", "agent identifier (required)")
    claimCmd.Flags().DurationVar(&claimDuration, "duration", 15*time.Minute, "lease duration")
    cobra.CheckErr(claimCmd.MarkFlagRequired("agent"))
    rootCmd.AddCommand(claimCmd)

    unclaimCmd.Flags().StringVar(&unclaimAgent, "agent", "", "agent identifier (required)")
    cobra.CheckErr(unclaimCmd.MarkFlagRequired("agent"))
    rootCmd.AddCommand(unclaimCmd)
}","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-01T00:38:27Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-01T00:38:27Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/pull/4"}

{"id":"spgr-uqx.1","title":"AuthoringBackend interface uses protobuf types, violating storage layer convention","description":"CLAUDE.md explicitly states: 'Storage interfaces use domain types, not protobuf types.' However, the new AuthoringBackend interface in internal/storage/authoring.go is full of protobuf types:

  StoreSparkOutput(ctx context.Context, slug string, output *specv1.SparkOutput) error
  StoreShapeOutput(ctx context.Context, slug string, output *specv1.ShapeOutput) error
  StoreSpecifyOutput(ctx context.Context, slug string, output *specv1.SpecifyOutput) error
  StoreDecomposeOutput(ctx context.Context, slug string, output *specv1.DecomposeOutput) ([]*specv1.Spec, error)
  StoreRedTeamFindings(ctx context.Context, slug string, findings []*specv1.RedTeamFinding) error
  StorePeripheralVision(ctx context.Context, slug string, items []*specv1.PeripheralVisionItem) error
  StoreConsistencyIssues(ctx context.Context, slug string, issues []*specv1.ConsistencyIssue) error
  StoreSimplicityFindings(ctx context.Context, slug string, findings []*specv1.SimplicityFinding) error
  StoreSafetyFlags(ctx context.Context, slug string, flags []*specv1.SafetyFlag) error
  StoreConstitutionViolations(ctx context.Context, slug string, violations []*specv1.ConstitutionViolation) error
  AmendSpec(ctx context.Context, slug, reason, targetStage string) (*specv1.Spec, error)

File: internal/storage/authoring.go

Every existing storage interface (Backend, GraphBackend, ClaimBackend, ConstitutionBackend) uses domain types. The AuthoringBackend breaks this contract by having specv1 proto types directly in the interface signatures. Domain structs should be defined in the storage package or authoring package, with the proto<->domain conversion happening at the server handler layer (or a dedicated mapper).

Note: The existing Backend interface also returns *specv1.Spec, so there is already precedent for Spec being a 'domain' type that happens to be proto-generated. The real concern is stage-output types (SparkOutput, ShapeOutput, etc.) which are purely transport types and have no reason to bleed into the storage layer boundary.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-01T23:06:32Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-01T23:06:32Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/pull/8"}

{"id":"spgr-0oj.20","title":"Spec.stage is a free-form string but AuthoringStage is a typed enum — dual representations create contract ambiguity","description":"The existing Spec message in proto/specgraph/v1/spec.proto (field 4) uses a free-form string for stage (e.g. 'spark | shape | specify | decompose | approved | in_progress | done'). The new authoring.proto introduces AuthoringStage as a typed enum. These two representations co-exist in the same package but are never linked.

This creates several contract risks:

1. The Spec.stage string comment includes 'in_progress' and 'done' as valid values, but AuthoringStage has no corresponding enum values. The enum is missing at least two documented valid stage values.

2. AmendResponse and ApproveResponse return AuthoringStage (enum), but GetSpec returns Spec with stage as a plain string. Consumers reading stage from GetSpec/ListSpecs get a string; consumers using Amend/Approve get an enum. The same logical field has two incompatible types depending on which RPC is called.

3. The stageToProto map in internal/server/authoring_handler.go (lines 303-309) converts string → enum for ApproveResponse/AmendResponse, but this mapping is one-directional — there is no inverse serialization back into a Spec's string stage field from an AuthoringStage enum value.

Recommended fix: Either (a) migrate Spec.stage to use AuthoringStage enum (breaking change requiring a migration plan for existing stored data), or (b) document explicitly that Spec.stage is the persistence representation and AuthoringStage is the authoring-funnel representation, and add 'IN_PROGRESS' and 'DONE' enum values to AuthoringStage to cover all documented string values. Without resolving this, the API contract is ambiguous for consumers who read and write stage via different RPCs.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-03T01:08:43Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-03T01:36:03Z","closed_at":"2026-03-03T01:36:03Z","close_reason":"Out of scope: Spec.stage as a free-form string is a pre-existing design from Slice 1. {"id":"spgr-a4b.45","title":"validateSlug regex rejects single-character slugs","description":"In internal/server/authoring_handler.go line 22, validSlugRe = regexp.MustCompile(`^[a-z0-9][a-z0-9_/-]*[a-z0-9]$`). The middle group [a-z0-9_/-]* requires at least one character between the required start/end characters, making the minimum valid slug length 3 (e.g. 'a-b'). Actually the middle group is * (zero-or-more) but the pattern requires two distinct anchored characters, so the minimum length is 2 (e.g. 'ab'). A single-character slug like 'a' will fail validation with 'invalid characters' even though it is a perfectly valid identifier. This is a silent usability bug — no test covers single-char slugs. Fix: change the regex to '^[a-z0-9]([a-z0-9_/-]*[a-z0-9])?$' to allow single-char slugs, or document that single-char slugs are intentionally rejected.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-03T04:24:04Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-03T04:24:04Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/pull/8"}

{"id":"spgr-a4b.42","title":"Amend handler does not enforce that only approved specs can be amended per ADR-003 authoring flow","description":"ADR-003 (docs/initial-design-session/specgraph-v1.0-draft-adr-003-decisions.md, Authoring Flow Impact section) states: 'Amendment: When a spec is amended, its decisions may need revision.' This implies amendment is specifically an operation on approved specs being reopened. The plan's AmendRequest description (plan line ~281-285) says 'Amend(AmendRequest) — rolling a spec back to an earlier stage.'

The implementation in internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go AmendSpec explicitly guards against amending already-approved specs: 'if spec.Stage == authoring.StageApproved { return nil, storage.ErrSpecAlreadyApproved }' (diff line ~4289). This contradicts the spec semantics: amendment is the mechanism to reopen an approved spec. The valid use case 'approved spec needs rework → amend back to shape' is explicitly blocked by this guard.

The ValidateAmendTransition in internal/authoring/stages.go also requires backward transitions only (forward transitions are rejected), which is correct. But the ErrSpecAlreadyApproved guard in AmendSpec runs BEFORE ValidateAmendTransition and rejects all amendments on approved specs without giving the transition validator a chance to evaluate.

Files: internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go (AmendSpec function, diff lines ~4283-4293).","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-03T04:23:52Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-03T04:23:52Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/pull/8"}

{"id":"spgr-a4b.6","title":"Safety scanner exposes internal regex patterns in API responses","description":"File: internal/authoring/safety.go, line ~1850

The `RunSafetyNet` function sets the `Description` field of each `SafetyFlagResult` to the raw matched regex string:

    Description: "matched pattern: " + sp.re.String()

This string is then serialized into the proto `SafetyFlag.description` field and returned to API callers in `SparkResponse.safety_flags`, `ShapeResponse.safety_flags`, and `SpecifyResponse.safety_flags`.

Exposing internal pattern implementation details is an information disclosure that violates defence-in-depth: it reveals the exact detection logic to an attacker, making it trivial to craft input that triggers safety flags (or avoids them) with knowledge of the internal pattern set.

Suggested fix: Replace the regex string with a human-readable, category-level description that does not reveal the matched pattern. For example: 'Potential security concern detected' rather than 'matched pattern: \\bhardcoded secret\\b'.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-03T04:22:19Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-03T04:22:19Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/pull/8"}

{"id":"spgr-a4b.7","title":"Duplicate FindingSeverity and SafetyCategory types across authoring and storage packages","description":"Two separate type definitions for conceptually identical concepts exist in the codebase:

1. authoring.FindingSeverity (int, values 1/2/3) in internal/authoring/safety.go:15
2. storage.FindingSeverity (string, values critical/warning/note) in internal/storage/authoring.go:82

Similarly:
1. authoring.SafetyCategory (string) in internal/authoring/safety.go:34
2. storage.SafetyCategory (string) in internal/storage/authoring.go:128

The two FindingSeverity types use different underlying kinds (int vs string) with different constant values, making the relationship confusing and requiring a mapping table (severityToProto) to bridge them. There is no compile-time relationship enforced between authoring.SeverityCritical (int=1) and storage.SeverityCritical (string='critical').

This creates a maintenance hazard: adding a new severity level requires editing both type definitions, the conversion map, the proto enum, and constants in two packages. A caller who misuses storage.FindingSeverity where authoring.FindingSeverity is expected will get a type error, but the conceptual duplication is still unnecessary.

Suggested fix: Consolidate FindingSeverity and SafetyCategory into a single canonical location (either the storage package as the persistence contract, or a new internal/domain package). The authoring package's scanner layer should convert from the canonical type to proto rather than defining its own parallel type hierarchy.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-03T04:22:21Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-03T04:22:21Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/pull/8"}

{"id":"spgr-a4b.8","title":"SafetyInput.Intent field comment is misleading for non-Spark use cases","description":"internal/authoring/safety.go:1737-1743 — The SafetyInput struct comment reads: 'Intent accepts any text for pattern scanning — its name reflects the Spark use case, but other handlers pass stage-appropriate text (e.g., risks in Shape).' This comment is attempting to explain away a naming mismatch rather than resolving it. The field name 'Intent' is actively misleading for Shape (where it receives Risks) and for Decompose (where it receives joined slice intents). This will confuse future contributors adding new stages. Consider renaming the field to 'Text' or 'Content', or documenting the mapping table (Spark→seed, Shape→risks, Specify→interface_contract, Decompose→slice intents) explicitly in the struct comment. The existing inline comment in authoring_handler.go at line 3108 repeats the same workaround explanation.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-03T04:22:21Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-03T04:22:21Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/pull/8"}

{"id":"spgr-a4b.23","title":"SafetyFlagResult.Description leaks internal regex implementation detail to callers","description":"In internal/authoring/safety.go:136, SafetyFlagResult.Description is populated with:

  Description: 'matched pattern: ' + sp.re.String()

This exposes the compiled regex string (e.g. 'matched pattern: \beval\(\b') directly in the domain result type and propagates it through to the proto response. The Description field on SafetyFlagResult is meant to be a human-readable explanation for the operator, not an implementation artifact.

A caller (or end user) seeing 'matched pattern: \bdrop table\b' instead of 'Potentially destructive DDL detected' gets worse UX and a leaked abstraction. The safetyPattern struct should carry a human-readable label alongside the regex, and Description should use that label.

This also makes tests fragile: tests that assert on Description values will break if the regex pattern changes, even if the semantic behavior is unchanged.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-03T04:22:56Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-03T04:22:56Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/pull/8"}

{"id":"spgr-a4b.14","title":"loadJSONFile (cmd/specgraph/util.go): no tests for malformed JSON or missing file","description":"cmd/specgraph/util.go introduces the generic loadJSONFile[T proto.Message] function used by shape, specify, decompose, and spark commands when --json-file is provided. This is a shared utility with three distinct failure modes:

1. File does not exist → os.ReadFile returns error
2. File exists but contains invalid JSON → protojson.Unmarshal returns error
3. File is valid JSON but the wrong schema for the target proto message type

None of these are tested in cmd/specgraph/cmd_test.go. The existing tests only exercise cobra argument validation (flag registration and positional arg counts).

While the CLI commands themselves require a running server to fully test, loadJSONFile is a pure function that can be tested in isolation using os.CreateTemp and a proto message. A malformed --json-file silently passes cobra parsing and only fails at runtime when RunE is called.

Suggested fix: Add unit tests in cmd/specgraph/util_test.go (or similar) that:
- Create a temp file with invalid JSON and verify loadJSONFile returns an error containing 'parse'
- Create a temp file with valid JSON for a known proto message (e.g. SparkOutput) and verify successful unmarshal
- Pass a nonexistent path and verify the error contains 'read'","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-03T04:22:36Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-03T04:22:36Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/pull/8"}

{"id":"spgr-s34.1","title":"[security] storeJSONProperty interpolates property name into Cypher query string","description":"File: internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go, lines 3990-3994

The storeJSONProperty function uses fmt.Sprintf to interpolate the property name directly into a Cypher query string:

    query := fmt.Sprintf(`
        MATCH (s:Spec {slug: $slug})
        SET s.%s = $data, s.updated_at = $updated_at
        RETURN s.slug
    `, property)

The slug and data values are correctly parameterized, but the property name is interpolated as a string literal into the query itself. Two defenses are in place: an allowlist check (allowedJSONProperties map) and a character validation loop. Both checks happen before the interpolation, so the actual risk of injection through a hot code path is low in the current implementation.

However, the defense is brittle:
1. If a future caller passes a property name that slips past both guards (e.g. a refactor accidentally uses a variable not populated from the allowlist), the query becomes injectable.
2. The allowlist and character check are internal to the function; callers at the storage interface level have no visibility into this constraint.
3. The character check iterates byte-by-byte over a string that has already passed the allowlist — if the allowlist is ever removed or bypassed, the character check alone does not prevent all injection vectors (e.g. a property name ending with a backtick or Cypher keyword boundary).

Recommended fix: Consider restructuring to avoid interpolation entirely by using a lookup table (map[string]string) of pre-built query templates, one per property name. This eliminates the runtime string interpolation path completely and makes the safe-by-construction nature explicit.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-04T01:22:26Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-04T01:22:26Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/pull/8"}

{"id":"spgr-s34.7","title":"[types] Duplicate FindingSeverity type across authoring and storage packages","description":"FindingSeverity is defined twice with different representations: storage.FindingSeverity (string, internal/storage/authoring.go:98) and authoring.FindingSeverity (int, internal/authoring/safety.go:17). The two are bridged by a manual lookup map (severityToStorage) and conversion function (ToStorageSeverity) in safety.go. This is a DRY violation and a maintenance hazard: adding a new severity level requires updating both type definitions, three constant blocks (storage, authoring, proto), and the bridge map. The justification in the comment ('authoring uses ordered ints for comparison; storage uses strings for JSON') is valid but the same result can be achieved with a single type. {"id":"spgr-s34.12","title":"Approve RPC hardcodes transition from decompose stage only, preventing approval from earlier stages","description":"In internal/server/authoring_handler.go the Approve handler calls TransitionStage with a hardcoded 'from' of StageDecompose:

{"id":"spgr-s34.42","title":"StoreDecomposeOutput: DEPENDS_ON edge to a non-existent sibling slug is not validated or tested","description":"In StoreDecomposeOutput (authoring.go:128-138), when a slice's DependsOn list references a sibling slice ID, a DEPENDS_ON edge is created via MERGE. This is a data-integrity gap: a mistyped DependsOn ID silently produces an incomplete graph with no error signal.

{"id":"spgr-s34.45","title":"StoreDecomposeOutput DEPENDS_ON edge creation silently references potentially non-existent sibling slugs","description":"In internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go, StoreDecomposeOutput iterates over each slice's DependsOn list and constructs a childSlug from the parent slug plus the dependency ID (e.g. 'parent/dep-id'). It then runs a MERGE DEPENDS_ON Cypher query.

However, there is no check that the target dependency spec actually exists before the MERGE. If a slice references a dependency ID that does not correspond to any slice in the current decompose output (a typo or a forward reference to a not-yet-created spec), the MATCH inside MERGE will find no 'to' node and the MERGE will create 0 rows — silently. No error is returned. The DEPENDS_ON edge is never created, the decompose operation succeeds, and the caller has no indication that a dependency reference was dropped.

Location: internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go, StoreDecomposeOutput(), DEPENDS_ON block (~lines 3850-3861 in diff).

Suggested fix: After the DEPENDS_ON MERGE query, check if records is empty and return an error identifying the missing dependency slug. Alternatively, validate all DependsOn references against the current slice ID list before executing any Cypher.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-04T01:24:37Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-04T01:24:37Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/pull/8"}

{"id":"spgr-s34.62","title":"AuthoringBackend PassWriter methods are stored in storage but never called from handler — dead API surface","description":"The AuthoringBackend interface in internal/storage/authoring.go includes a PassWriter sub-interface with 6 methods:
  StoreRedTeamFindings, StorePeripheralVision, StoreConsistencyIssues, StoreSimplicityFindings, StoreSafetyFlags, StoreConstitutionViolations

All 6 methods are implemented in memgraph/authoring.go and all 6 are implemented in the test fake (fakeAuthoringBackend in authoring_handler_test.go).

However, none of these 6 methods are ever called from the authoring handler. The handler runs safety checks (RunSafetyNet) and returns results in the response, but does not persist them via the storage interface. The Decompose handler even has a TODO comment acknowledging that simplicity_check and constitution_check are not wired up.

This creates an API contract mismatch: the storage interface promises persistence of these findings, clients see the findings in the response, but nothing is stored. If a client later queries a spec expecting to retrieve historical findings, they will not be found.

This is RISKY, not BREAKING (the API is new), but it establishes a misleading contract from day one.

Suggested fix: Either (1) call the PassWriter methods from the handler after computing findings (StoreSafetyFlags, StoreRedTeamFindings, etc.), or (2) remove PassWriter from AuthoringBackend until it is ready to be implemented, to avoid shipping a dead interface. Option 2 is safer for the initial API contract.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-04T01:25:20Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-04T01:25:20Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/pull/8"}

{"id":"spgr-s34.72","title":"SupersedeSpec uses two-phase check-then-act with TOCTOU window between GetSpec calls and query","description":"File: internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go, SupersedeSpec (lines ~170-200 of authoring.go).

`SupersedeSpec` performs two separate `GetSpec` calls to validate both specs exist, then runs the mutating query. Between the existence checks and the mutation, either spec could be deleted by a concurrent operation, resulting in the combined MATCH query returning no records.

In that case the code returns `ErrSpecNotFound`, which is correct — but the preceding error message attributes the failure to the original slug rather than the actual missing one, making diagnosis harder.

More critically, without a transaction wrapping all three operations (two GET + one MUTATE), this is a TOCTOU race. The `RunInTransaction` capability exists on `Store` but is not used here.

Fix: Either wrap `SupersedeSpec` in a `RunInTransaction` call, or collapse the two existence checks into the mutating Cypher query using MATCH with explicit failure handling (e.g. using OPTIONAL MATCH + CASE to detect which spec is missing).","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-04T01:26:08Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-04T01:26:08Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/pull/8"}

{"id":"spgr-s34.43","title":"GetPrompts returns CodeInvalidArgument for APPROVED stage but proto doc says this instead of consistent error code","description":"In authoring.proto, the GetPromptsRequest comment says:
  'The APPROVED stage has no prompts; requesting it returns INVALID_ARGUMENT.'

The handler in authoring_handler.go implements this via the protoToStage map — APPROVED maps to storage.AuthoringStage, and PromptsToProto returns nil for 'approved', triggering CodeInvalidArgument.

However, CodeInvalidArgument is semantically wrong for this case. The stage value APPROVED is a valid enum value; the client's request is well-formed. The issue is that no prompts exist for that stage. CodeNotFound or CodeUnimplemented would be more appropriate.

Using CodeInvalidArgument for a valid enum value is confusing for clients: they would normally see INVALID_ARGUMENT for a malformed request, not for a valid request that simply has no data associated with it. This is an API design issue that could lead to incorrect client error handling.

Suggested fix: Either return CodeNotFound('no prompts defined for stage APPROVED') or return an empty GetPromptsResponse{} (zero-length prompts slice) and let clients handle the empty case. The proto comment should be updated to match whichever behavior is chosen. If returning empty is acceptable, update GetPromptsResponse to note that an empty prompts list means no prompts are defined for the stage.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-04T01:24:33Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-04T01:24:33Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/pull/8"}

{"id":"spgr-s34.27","title":"AmendResponse uses int32 version but Spec message uses int32 version — type is fine but semantics are undocumented","description":"proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto AmendResponse.version (field 3) is described as 'Monotonically increasing amendment count for this spec.' {"id":"spgr-s34.2","title":"[security] No authorization/authentication on any AuthoringService RPC","description":"File: internal/server/authoring_handler.go and cmd/specgraph/serve.go

All 8 RPCs (Spark, Shape, Specify, Decompose, Approve, Amend, Supersede, GetPrompts) are registered with no authentication or authorization middleware:

    // serve.go line 308
    server.RegisterAuthoringService(mux, store, store)

    // RegisterAuthoringService in authoring_handler.go line 2827
    path, h := specgraphv1connect.NewAuthoringServiceHandler(handler)
    mux.Handle(path, h)

Any unauthenticated caller on the network can:
- Create arbitrary spec nodes (Spark) with any slug
- Advance or roll back any spec through the authoring funnel (Shape, Specify, Decompose, Approve, Amend)
- Mark any spec as superseded (Supersede)
- This is true for all existing services (GraphService, ClaimService, ConstitutionService) as well, but the authoring funnel is new in this PR and expands the attack surface

Severity classified as important (not critical) because this appears to be an early-phase internal tool without documented external deployment, but the pattern should be addressed before any production or shared deployment.

Recommended fix: Add a middleware interceptor (ConnectRPC interceptor pattern) that enforces authentication at minimum — even a simple API key or mutual TLS check — before any handler is invoked. The interceptor can be added once at mux registration time and applied to all services.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-04T01:22:42Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-04T01:22:42Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/pull/8"}

{"id":"spgr-s34.26","title":"SupersedeSpec is not transactional: partial failure leaves graph in inconsistent state","description":"SupersedeSpec in internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go performs three distinct operations without a transaction:
1. GetSpec(slug) — read to verify source exists
2. GetSpec(supersededBy) — read to verify target exists
3. executeQuery() — SET stage + CREATE SUPERSEDES edge

If the Memgraph write in step 3 fails after both reads succeed, the spec stage is not rolled back and no SUPERSEDES edge exists. The graph is silently left in a partial state. The handler in authoring_handler.go calls this method without wrapping it in RunInTransaction, and there is no compensating logic or logging.

Location: internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go, SupersedeSpec() (~lines 3900-3924 in diff); internal/server/authoring_handler.go, Supersede() (~line 2790).

Suggested fix: Wrap the entire SupersedeSpec operation in a transaction, or at minimum add error-level logging on partial failure. The handler should also attempt to use txBackend if available, matching the pattern used by Spark/Shape/Specify/Decompose.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-04T01:23:56Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-04T01:23:56Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/pull/8"}

{"id":"spgr-s34.35","title":"TransitionStage check-then-act race: secondary check query runs outside the original transaction scope","description":"In internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go, TransitionStage() performs the stage update as a single Cypher query. When records is empty it runs a second checkQuery to distinguish 'spec not found' from 'wrong stage'. The check query is executed via executeQuery() which, inside RunInTransaction, routes through the managed transaction. However the original update (which returned 0 records) has already completed within that transaction scope; the check query is a separate read under the same transaction, which may not see the same consistent snapshot depending on isolation level.

More critically, when TransitionStage is called *outside* a transaction (the non-tx fallback path in the handler), the primary write and the diagnostic read are completely separate autocommit operations, creating a TOCTOU window where another writer could change the spec's stage between the two queries. In that window, the error returned may incorrectly say 'wrong stage' when the spec was concurrently deleted, or vice versa — leading to a misleading error message that obscures the real failure.

Location: internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go, TransitionStage(), lines ~3783-3795 in diff.

Suggested fix: Remove the diagnostic second query and return a generic 'spec not found or wrong stage' error. The caller already has the expected stage in the error message. Alternatively, use a single Cypher query that returns a status discriminator (e.g. CASE WHEN ... THEN 'not_found' ELSE 'wrong_stage' END) so the discrimination happens atomically.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-04T01:24:13Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-04T01:24:13Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/pull/8"}

{"id":"spgr-s34.51","title":"stage field in Spec proto uses free-form string but AuthoringStage enum exists — inconsistency creates contract ambiguity","description":"The existing Spec message in proto/specgraph/v1/spec.proto uses a free-form string for stage:

{"id":"spgr-s34.70","title":"amend_reason written to spec node but never cleared or read back in any query","description":"File: internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go, AmendSpec query (line 3942 of diff / line ~120 of file).

{"id":"spgr-s34.73","title":"Spark handler uses msg.Output.GetSeed() as spec intent, conflating authoring seed with spec intent","description":"File: internal/server/authoring_handler.go, Spark handler (lines 63, 78).

`CreateSpec` is called with `msg.Output.GetSeed()` as the `intent` argument. The `seed` field in `SparkOutput` is described as 'Raw idea or problem statement provided by the author.' — a first-pass free-form input. The `intent` field on `Spec` is the authoritative purpose statement for the spec.

{"id":"spgr-s34.49","title":"Dead code: GetSpec nil-result branch in StoreDecomposeOutput is unreachable","description":"File: internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go, lines 114-117.

The `else if existing == nil` branch after checking `getErr != nil` is unreachable. `GetSpec` either returns a non-nil `*specv1.Spec` on success or an error (including `storage.ErrSpecNotFound`). This is a logic bug: if somehow the nil path were ever reached, the newly-created spec would be created without the intent from `sl.Intent` because the nil branch duplicates the creation call identically. More importantly, the dead branch suggests a misunderstanding of the API contract.

Fix: Remove the `else if existing == nil` block entirely. {"id":"spgr-s34.66","title":"DecompositionSlice.id field has no validation — empty or duplicate IDs create broken child spec slugs","description":"The DecompositionSlice message (proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto) has:
  string id = 1; // Stable identifier for this slice within the parent spec.

The Decompose handler in authoring_handler.go calls decomposeOutputToDomain which maps slice IDs directly. Then StoreDecomposeOutput in memgraph/authoring.go creates child slugs via:
  childSlug := fmt.Sprintf("%s/%s", slug, sl.ID)

There is no validation that:
1. sl.ID is non-empty (empty ID → child slug becomes 'parent-spec/')
2. sl.ID values are unique within the output (duplicate IDs → two slices create the same child slug)
3. sl.ID values are valid slug characters (the Decompose handler calls decomposeOutputToDomain before validateSlug for child IDs)

For duplicate IDs: the first call creates the child, the second finds it exists via GetSpec and reuses it — creating a partially wrong dependency graph. For empty IDs: 'parent-spec/' is a syntactically invalid slug that would pass validateSlugRe since it contains '/'.

Wait — actually '/' alone would not pass validSlugRe (^[a-z0-9][a-z0-9_/-]*[a-z0-9]$) but 'parent-spec/' ends with '/' which fails the trailing [a-z0-9] requirement. However the slice ID itself is never validated with validateSlug.

Suggested fix: Add validation in the Decompose handler that each DecompositionSlice.id is non-empty, unique within the output, and matches valid slug character rules. Return CodeInvalidArgument with a descriptive message including the problematic slice ID.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-04T01:25:35Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-04T01:25:35Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/pull/8"}

{"id":"spgr-34l.38","title":"[api] FindingSeverity enum is shared across unrelated message types creating coupling risk","description":"The FindingSeverity enum at proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto lines 39-47 is used as the severity field in three distinct message types: SafetyFlag (line 206), RedTeamFinding (line 157), and ConstitutionViolation (line 195). These are semantically distinct concepts:

- SafetyFlag.severity: indicates whether a safety concern requires immediate action
- RedTeamFinding.severity: indicates whether a challenge blocks spec advancement  
- ConstitutionViolation.severity: indicates whether a constraint violation blocks advancement

While the CRITICAL/WARNING/NOTE values happen to map sensibly to all three today, coupling these types to a single shared enum creates brittleness: if the severity model needs to diverge for one type (e.g., safety flags need a FATAL level), changing the enum affects all consumers of all three types.

This is a proto API design concern because enum values in proto3 are shared across the namespace and cannot be independently evolved per message.

Suggested fix: Consider using separate enums per domain concept (SafetyFlagSeverity, FindingSeverity, ViolationSeverity) even if they start with identical values. This follows the proto best practice of preferring redundancy over coupling at the cost of future flexibility.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-04T02:16:46Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-04T02:16:46Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/pull/8"}

{"id":"spgr-34l.43","title":"[api] Spark RPC creates spec and advances stage atomically but other stage RPCs do not create the spec — missing symmetry","description":"The Spark RPC at internal/server/authoring_handler.go line 2547 calls CreateSpec() as part of its transaction, creating the spec node. All other stage RPCs (Shape, Specify, Decompose) only call TransitionStage() and StoreXOutput() — they assume the spec already exists.

This creates an implicit pre-condition on Shape/Specify/Decompose that is not documented in the proto: callers must have previously called Spark (or created the spec via SpecService.CreateSpec) before calling these RPCs. If the spec does not exist, they receive CodeNotFound.

However, the SparkRequest proto comment (line 225-234) says 'Spark creates a new spec and enters the spark stage', suggesting Spark is the canonical entry point. But clients could reasonably try to call Shape directly on an existing spec that was created via SpecService.CreateSpec.

The behavior when a spec exists at the wrong stage (e.g., calling Shape on a spec at 'decompose') is CodeFailedPrecondition. But the behavior when the spec does not exist at all is CodeNotFound, and Shape's error path goes through h.stageError() which does correctly map ErrSpecNotFound to CodeNotFound.

Suggested fix: Document in the proto service comments that Spark is the required entry point to the authoring funnel. The Shape/Specify/Decompose/Approve/Amend RPCs all require the spec to exist. Add explicit 'Returns NOT_FOUND if the spec does not exist' to each RPC's comment for discoverability.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-04T02:17:00Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-04T02:17:00Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/pull/8"}

{"id":"spgr-34l.12","title":"[types] Duplicate FindingSeverity type with incompatible underlying types across packages","description":"Two separate FindingSeverity types are defined with different underlying types, same constant names, and same semantic intent:

1. internal/authoring/safety.go: type FindingSeverity int, with SeverityCritical=1, SeverityWarning=2, SeverityNote=3
2. internal/storage/authoring.go: type FindingSeverity string, with SeverityCritical="critical", SeverityWarning="warning", SeverityNote="note"

These are two wholly distinct types despite identical exported names. storage.SafetyFlag.Severity is storage.FindingSeverity (string), while authoring.SafetyFlagResult.Severity is authoring.FindingSeverity (int). The int-based type is used internally for comparison/deduplication logic (m.Severity < existing.Severity), while the string-based type is used for JSON serialisation in storage domain types.

This creates confusion: callers of SafetyResultsToProto must use authoring.FindingSeverity, but SafetyFlag in storage uses storage.FindingSeverity. The types are not interchangeable and require explicit conversion. The severityToProto map in safety.go maps authoring.FindingSeverity (int) to proto, but storage.FindingSeverity (string) is what ends up in persisted and serialised flags.

Suggested fix: consolidate on a single FindingSeverity type. Since it is persisted and serialised, string is the more appropriate underlying type. The int ordering for deduplication can use an explicit rank map (map[FindingSeverity]int) instead of relying on integer value comparison.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-04T02:15:41Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-04T02:15:41Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/pull/8"}

{"id":"spgr-34l.9","title":"Safety flags computed in RPC handlers but never persisted to storage","description":"In internal/server/authoring_handler.go, every RPC handler (Spark, Shape, Specify, Decompose) computes safety flags and returns them to the caller:

    safetyFlags := authoring.RunSafetyNet(&authoring.SafetyInput{Text: msg.Output.GetSeed()})
    return connect.NewResponse(&specv1.SparkResponse{
        SafetyFlags: authoring.SafetyResultsToProto(safetyFlags),
        ...
    }), nil

However, StoreSafetyFlags is defined on the AuthoringBackend interface (internal/storage/authoring.go) and the Memgraph implementation exists (internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go StoreSafetyFlags), but none of the handlers ever call h.store.StoreSafetyFlags(). The safety analysis runs on every request, the results are returned to the client, but they are never durably recorded in the graph.

This means:
- Audit trails of safety concerns are lost between requests
- Downstream consumers (analytics, constitution check integration) can never read persisted flags
- If a client drops the response, the safety finding is silently gone

The fakeAuthoringBackend in authoring_handler_test.go has StoreSafetyFlags as a no-op (returns nil), confirming this code path is never exercised in the handler.

Fix: After the transaction in each handler, call h.store.StoreSafetyFlags() with the computed flags, wrapping the error and logging it at minimum (or returning it if it should be blocking).","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-04T02:15:30Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-04T02:15:30Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/pull/8"}

{"id":"spgr-34l.11","title":"[security] No length cap on free-text RPC fields (reason, seed, interface_contract) — unbounded writes to graph","description":"The slug field has a 256-character cap (maxSlugLength) and regex validation, but the following free-text RPC fields have no length limit:

- AmendRequest.reason (written to s.amend_reason on the Cypher node)
- SupersedeRequest.reason (written as a property on the SUPERSEDES edge)
- SparkOutput.seed (stored as JSON in spark_output property)
- SpecifyOutput.interface_contract (stored as JSON in specify_output property)
- All repeated string fields in ShapeOutput, SpecifyOutput, DecomposeOutput

Locations:
  internal/server/authoring_handler.go:2707 (Amend reason check — only checks empty)
  internal/server/authoring_handler.go:2733 (Supersede reason — no check at all)
  internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go:4031-4037 (AmendSpec writes reason verbatim)

Impact: A caller can submit multi-megabyte strings that are marshalled to JSON and stored as node properties in Memgraph. This can cause:
1. Excessive memory use during JSON marshalling in the server process.
2. Oversized property values in Memgraph nodes.
3. Slow safety-net regex scanning of huge inputs (RunSafetyNet joins all strings and runs several regexes).

Suggested fix: Add a maximum length constant (e.g. maxReasonLength = 4096) and validate all free-text fields at the handler boundary before any storage or safety-net operations.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-04T02:15:41Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-04T02:15:41Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/pull/8"}

{"id":"spgr-34l.18","title":"[api] GetPrompts for APPROVED stage returns CodeNotFound instead of CodeInvalidArgument as documented","description":"The GetPromptsRequest proto comment at proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto line 378 states: 'The APPROVED stage has no prompts; requesting it returns INVALID_ARGUMENT.' However, the handler at internal/server/authoring_handler.go line 2764 returns CodeNotFound when the stage is valid but has no prompts, which is what happens for APPROVED.

The test at authoring_handler_test.go line 3398 (TestAuthoringHandler_GetPrompts_ApprovedStage) asserts CodeNotFound, confirming the implementation disagrees with the proto comment.

This is a contract violation: consumers reading the proto documentation will write code expecting INVALID_ARGUMENT (4) for APPROVED, but will receive NOT_FOUND (5). This is a behavioral change risk for any client generated from the proto spec.

Suggested fix: Either (a) update the handler to return CodeInvalidArgument when Stage == AUTHORING_STAGE_APPROVED before the protoToStage lookup, or (b) update the proto comment to say 'returns NOT_FOUND'. Option (a) is preferred because APPROVED is a known, documented special case, not a missing resource.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-04T02:15:55Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-04T02:15:55Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/pull/8"}

{"id":"spgr-34l.26","title":"[comments] GetPromptsRequest proto doc says APPROVED returns INVALID_ARGUMENT but handler returns NOT_FOUND","description":"proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto line ~4975:

The comment on GetPromptsRequest reads:
  'The APPROVED stage has no prompts; requesting it returns INVALID_ARGUMENT.'

However, the handler at internal/server/authoring_handler.go (TestAuthoringHandler_GetPrompts_ApprovedStage test and the GetPrompts implementation) shows that an APPROVED stage request actually returns CodeNotFound (not CodeInvalidArgument). UNSPECIFIED returns CodeInvalidArgument; APPROVED returns CodeNotFound because it passes the protoToStage lookup but then finds no prompts defined.

This inaccuracy could mislead client authors who implement error handling based on the proto comment and expect INVALID_ARGUMENT from a known-but-empty stage.

Suggested fix: Update the comment to:
  'The APPROVED stage has no prompts; requesting it returns NOT_FOUND.
   The UNSPECIFIED stage returns INVALID_ARGUMENT.'","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-04T02:16:10Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-04T02:16:10Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/pull/8"}

{"id":"spgr-34l.3","title":"validateSlug test coverage missing slug-length boundary and exact 256-char slug","description":"The validateSlug function in internal/server/authoring_handler.go (line ~2502) enforces maxSlugLength=256. The handler tests at internal/server/authoring_handler_test.go test empty-slug, path-traversal (..), and invalid-char cases, but no test exercises the 256-char boundary.

Missing cases:
- A slug of exactly 256 characters (should be valid per the current implementation: len(slug) > maxSlugLength means 256 is accepted).
- A slug of 257 characters (should return CodeInvalidArgument).

This matters because the boundary condition is off-by-one: the guard is '>' not '>=' so a 256-char slug passes today. A future change to '>=' would be a silent regression with no test to catch it.

Suggested fix: add two table-driven subtests to TestAuthoringHandler_Spark_* covering exactly-256 and 257-char slugs, asserting the expected error code each time.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-04T02:15:01Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-04T02:15:01Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/pull/8"}

{"id":"spgr-34l.10","title":"Spark handler does not verify posture field is threaded into safety/pass selection","description":"The SparkRequest, ShapeRequest, SpecifyRequest, and DecomposeRequest proto messages all carry a posture field (internal/server/authoring_handler.go, via proto at proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto). The authoring package exposes ResolvePosture and PassesForStage which use posture to select which analytical passes run automatically versus offered.

However, the handler never reads req.Msg.Posture — it calls RunSafetyNet unconditionally without posture-aware pass selection. The authoring_handler_test.go has no test that sends a non-UNSPECIFIED posture and verifies different passes or behaviour results.

This is an important gap: the posture field is part of the public API contract, it is documented in the proto, and the authoring package has well-tested posture logic — but there is no test verifying the handler honours it. When the handler is wired to use posture (planned), the absence of a test baseline means the feature could be shipped with the posture silently ignored.

Suggested fix: add at least one test per affected RPC (Spark, Shape, Specify, Decompose) that sets Posture = DRIVE vs SUPPORT and asserts different pass outputs or next_prompts counts, even if the handler currently ignores posture (document the TODO in the test).","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-04T02:15:39Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-04T02:15:39Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/pull/8"}

{"id":"spgr-34l.22","title":"GetPrompts handler has no test for each individual valid stage returning correct prompt names","description":"TestAuthoringHandler_GetPrompts in internal/server/authoring_handler_test.go (line ~3056) only exercises AUTHORING_STAGE_SPARK, verifying three specific prompt names. The other valid stages (SHAPE, SPECIFY, DECOMPOSE) have no handler-level GetPrompts test; neither does the error path for AUTHORING_STAGE_APPROVED (tested separately) cover the stage mapping logic for non-spark stages.

The promptRegistry in internal/authoring/prompts.go is unit-tested via prompts_test.go, but there is no end-to-end assertion that the handler correctly maps proto enums to domain stage strings for shape/specify/decompose and that the response contains the expected prompt names.

If the protoToStage lookup map in authoring_handler.go were to lose an entry (e.g. AUTHORING_STAGE_SPECIFY missing), the handler would return CodeInvalidArgument for valid input, with no test to catch the regression.

Suggested fix: add parameterised subtests for SHAPE, SPECIFY, and DECOMPOSE in TestAuthoringHandler_GetPrompts, asserting resp.Msg.Prompts is non-empty and that prompts[0].Stage matches the requested stage enum.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-04T02:16:03Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-04T02:16:03Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/pull/8"}

{"id":"spgr-34l.35","title":"TestAuthoringHandler_GetPrompts duplicates server/mux setup instead of using newAuthoringClient helper","description":"Three tests in internal/server/authoring_handler_test.go inline the same mux+httptest.NewServer+defer srv.Close() boilerplate instead of using the newAuthoringClient helper defined at line ~3047:

- TestAuthoringHandler_GetPrompts (line ~3056): inlines mux setup, srv := httptest.NewServer, defer srv.Close
- TestAuthoringHandler_GetPrompts_UnspecifiedStage (line ~3382): same
- TestAuthoringHandler_GetPrompts_ApprovedStage (line ~3398): same

newAuthoringClient already does exactly this and registers cleanup via t.Cleanup. These three tests should use it instead:

  client := newAuthoringClient(t, &fakeAuthoringBackend{}, &fakeBackend{})

This eliminates 6-8 lines of duplicated setup per test and ensures consistent cleanup semantics (t.Cleanup vs defer, which behave differently in subtests).","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-04T02:16:36Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-04T02:16:36Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/pull/8"}

{"id":"spgr-34l.7","title":"StoreDecomposeOutput duplicate-slice-ID and unknown-dependency errors have no integration test","description":"memgraph.StoreDecomposeOutput in internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go (lines ~3903-3951) validates two constraints before writing:
1. Empty slice ID returns an error (line ~3906).
2. Duplicate slice ID returns an error (line ~3909-3910).
3. A DependsOn referencing a sibling ID not in the current output returns an error (line ~3938-3939).

The integration tests in internal/storage/memgraph/authoring_test.go cover the happy path, idempotency, and missing parent, but none of the three validation errors above are exercised against a real Memgraph instance.

If the validation logic were accidentally removed (e.g. during a refactor of the slice-building loop), the integration tests would not catch it. The unit-level handler tests use a fake backend and therefore also miss these paths.

Suggested additions to authoring_test.go:
- TestStoreDecomposeOutput_EmptySliceID: output with a slice where ID == ''.
- TestStoreDecomposeOutput_DuplicateSliceID: two slices with the same ID.
- TestStoreDecomposeOutput_UnknownDependency: slice with DependsOn pointing to a non-sibling ID.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-04T02:15:26Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-04T02:15:26Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/pull/8"}

{"id":"spgr-34l.28","title":"TransitionStage integration test does not verify backward (amend-via-TransitionStage) or approved guard paths","description":"TestTransitionStage in internal/storage/memgraph/authoring_test.go (line ~4133) covers: initial spark->shape, shape->specify, and a skip-forward error (specify->approved). TestTransitionStage_WrongStage covers the wrong-current-stage error path.

Missing integration test cases:
1. TransitionStage called with from = 'approved' (i.e., the ErrSpecAlreadyApproved guard at authoring.go line ~3842) — this guard fires before ValidateTransition and is distinct from the wrong-stage path.
2. TransitionStage on a slug that does not exist (ErrSpecNotFound path via the check query at line ~3864).

Both of these are important error paths that have meaningful storage-level behavior. Without tests, a change that accidentally removes either guard would produce no test failures.

Suggested fix:
- TestTransitionStage_AlreadyApproved: create a spec, advance to approved, call TransitionStage with from='approved', assert ErrorIs(err, storage.ErrSpecAlreadyApproved).
- TestTransitionStage_SpecNotFound: call TransitionStage on a non-existent slug, assert ErrorIs(err, storage.ErrSpecNotFound).","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-04T02:16:16Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-04T02:16:16Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/pull/8"}

{"id":"spgr-34l.24","title":"[types] Integration tests bypass typed AuthoringStage by passing raw string literals to TransitionStage","description":"The memgraph integration tests in internal/storage/memgraph/authoring_test.go call TransitionStage with raw string literals rather than typed constants or named AuthoringStage values:

    err = store.TransitionStage(ctx, "funnel-test", "spark", "shape")
    err = store.TransitionStage(ctx, "funnel-test", "shape", "specify")
    err = store.AmendSpec(ctx, "amend-test", "need to reconsider scope", "shape")

TransitionStage signature is: TransitionStage(ctx, slug string, from, to storage.AuthoringStage). Go allows passing untyped string literals to a string-based named type, so this compiles. However, it means a typo like "shaep" would not be caught at compile time in the test, and the tests fail to demonstrate the typed-constant usage pattern that the rest of the production code follows.

Suggested fix: use storage.AuthoringStage(authoring.StageShape) or, once the constants are typed as shown in the companion finding, use the constants directly. This makes tests more resilient and exemplifies correct usage.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-04T02:16:09Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-04T02:16:09Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/pull/8"}
